terms.title: UEN
terms.shortDef: A 9 or 10-character identifier issued by ACRA to every Singapore-registered business entity.
terms.definition: The Unique Entity Number (UEN) is the standard identification number for any business entity registered in Singapore. Issued by ACRA, it is permanent for the life of the entity and used for all interactions with government agencies (IRAS, MOM, CPF, MAS, SFA), banks, and other businesses. UEN format depends on entity type: Singapore-incorporated companies receive a 10-character UEN ending in a letter (e.g., 202012889R), while sole proprietorships and partnerships receive a 9-character ROB number (e.g., 53412345A).
terms.example: When you incorporate a Pte Ltd through ACRA, you receive your UEN immediately upon approval - typically within hours. The UEN appears on your Business Profile and is required when opening a bank account, registering for GST, or applying for any government licence.

terms.title: ACRA
terms.shortDef: Singapore's national regulator for businesses, public accountants, and corporate service providers.
terms.definition: The Accounting and Corporate Regulatory Authority (ACRA) is the statutory board under the Ministry of Finance responsible for regulating business entities, public accountants, and Corporate Service Providers (CSPs) in Singapore. ACRA administers the Companies Act, the Business Names Registration Act, and the Limited Liability Partnerships Act. All Singapore companies must file with ACRA at incorporation and lodge annual returns and other prescribed filings via the BizFile+ portal.
terms.example: Karman is an ACRA-Registered Filing Agent (FA), authorised to lodge incorporations, annual returns, and other filings on behalf of clients. ACRA's UEN for Karman appears on every filing receipt.

terms.title: IRAS
terms.shortDef: Singapore's tax authority, responsible for assessing and collecting corporate tax, GST, personal income tax, and stamp duty.
terms.definition: The Inland Revenue Authority of Singapore (IRAS) is the statutory board under the Ministry of Finance that administers Singapore's tax system. IRAS assesses and collects corporate income tax, individual income tax, Goods and Services Tax (GST), property tax, stamp duty, and various other taxes. Companies interact with IRAS primarily for ECI filing, Form C-S/C corporate tax return filing, GST returns, and withholding tax.
terms.example: After your company's first financial year ends, you must file an Estimated Chargeable Income (ECI) with IRAS within 3 months, then submit Form C-S or Form C by 30 November of the following year.

terms.title: BizFile+
terms.shortDef: ACRA's online filing portal for company incorporation, annual returns, and statutory filings.
terms.definition: BizFile+ is the official online portal operated by ACRA where all Singapore business filings are lodged. It covers incorporation, name reservations, change of company particulars, annual returns, charges (mortgages and debentures), and corporate restructuring filings. Most filings can be lodged directly by company officers using Singpass; foreign founders typically engage an ACRA-Registered Filing Agent to lodge on their behalf.
terms.example: When Karman files your annual return, the filing is lodged through BizFile+. You receive an email confirmation with the official receipt the moment ACRA accepts the filing.

terms.title: SSIC Code
terms.shortDef: A 5-digit code that identifies a company's primary business activity at ACRA.
terms.definition: Every Singapore company must declare at least one Singapore Standard Industrial Classification (SSIC) code at incorporation. The code is a 5-digit number that maps to a specific industry classification under SSIC 2020. A company can declare up to 2 codes (primary and secondary). SSIC codes determine eligibility for certain grants and tax incentives, and some codes (financial services, F&B, education) trigger downstream licensing requirements.
terms.example: A SaaS startup typically picks 62019 (Other software development) as its primary code. A food delivery business might pick 47913 (Online sale of food and beverages) plus 56122 (Food caterers) as a secondary.

terms.title: AGM
terms.shortDef: A yearly shareholders' meeting where the company's financial statements are presented for approval.
terms.definition: An Annual General Meeting (AGM) is a mandatory yearly meeting of shareholders of a Singapore company, where directors present the audited (or unaudited) financial statements for approval. Under Singapore's Companies Act, a private company must hold its first AGM within 6 months of its first financial year end (FYE), and subsequent AGMs at intervals not exceeding 15 months. Many private companies dispense with AGMs by passing a written resolution under Section 175A.
terms.example: If your company's FYE is 31 December 2026, the AGM (if held) must occur by 30 June 2027, and the annual return must be filed within 7 months of FYE - by 31 July 2027.

terms.title: EGM
terms.shortDef: Any shareholders' meeting held outside the AGM cycle, called to decide on time-sensitive matters.
terms.definition: An Extraordinary General Meeting (EGM) is any general meeting of shareholders that is not the AGM. EGMs are convened to decide on matters that cannot wait until the next AGM - examples include share allotments, capital reductions, removal of directors, amendments to the Constitution, or approval of major transactions. EGMs require notice of at least 14 days for ordinary resolutions and 21 days for special resolutions, unless shareholders agree to short notice.
terms.example: If shareholders need to approve a new share issuance to a Series A investor, the company will typically convene an EGM (or pass a written resolution in lieu) before allotting the shares.

terms.title: FYE
terms.shortDef: The date a company's financial year closes - all tax and reporting deadlines run from this date.
terms.definition: The Financial Year End (FYE) is the closing date of a company's accounting and tax year. Singapore companies are free to choose any FYE; the most common is 31 December (calendar year), but 31 March, 30 June, and 30 September are also widely used. The FYE drives every downstream deadline: AGM (if held) within 6 months, annual return within 7 months, ECI within 3 months, and Form C-S/C by 30 November of the following calendar year. The first FYE can be up to 18 months after incorporation.
terms.example: A company incorporated on 1 March 2026 may choose its first FYE as 31 December 2026 (10 months) or 31 March 2027 (13 months) or any date up to 31 August 2027 (18 months).

terms.title: Annual Return
terms.shortDef: An ACRA filing that confirms a company's officers, shareholders, share capital, and registered address.
terms.definition: The Annual Return is a mandatory ACRA filing that every Singapore company must lodge each year. It is a statutory snapshot of the company - directors, shareholders, share capital, registered address, and financial statement particulars. The deadline is 7 months after the FYE for private companies and 5 months for listed companies. Late filing attracts ACRA late lodgement fees of S$300+ and can lead to director disqualification under repeat offence rules.
terms.example: If your FYE is 31 December 2026, your annual return must be filed with ACRA by 31 July 2027. Missing the deadline triggers an automatic S$300 late lodgement fee and a composition fine.

terms.title: ECI
terms.shortDef: An IRAS declaration of a company's estimated taxable income, due 3 months after the FYE.
terms.definition: Estimated Chargeable Income (ECI) is an IRAS filing where a company declares its estimated taxable income for the financial year. The deadline is 3 months after the FYE. ECI filing is mandatory unless the company qualifies for the ECI waiver (revenue below S$5 million AND ECI is nil). Filing ECI early unlocks instalment payment plans for the corporate tax due, easing cash flow.
terms.example: If your FYE is 31 December 2026, your ECI must be filed with IRAS by 31 March 2027. If your revenue is under S$5M and you have nil chargeable income, you are exempt from filing.

terms.title: Form C-S / Form C
terms.fullName: Corporate Tax Return (Form C-S, Form C-S Lite, or Form C)
terms.shortDef: The annual corporate income tax return filed with IRAS, due by 30 November of the following year.
terms.definition: Form C-S, Form C-S Lite, or Form C is the annual corporate income tax return filed with IRAS. Smaller companies (revenue below S$5M, all-Singapore income, no special claims) qualify for Form C-S Lite, the simplest version. Companies with revenue under S$5M qualifying for the simpler form file Form C-S; everyone else files the full Form C with audited financials, tax computation, and supporting schedules. The deadline is always 30 November following the relevant Year of Assessment.
terms.example: For a company with FYE 31 December 2026 (Year of Assessment 2027), the corporate tax return must be filed by 30 November 2027.

terms.title: Corporate Tax
terms.shortDef: A flat 17% tax on chargeable income, with partial exemption schemes that reduce effective rates for SMEs.
terms.definition: Singapore's headline corporate tax rate is 17%, applied to chargeable income (taxable profit). New Start-Up companies enjoy the Start-Up Tax Exemption (SUTE) for the first 3 Years of Assessment: 75% exemption on first S$100K and 50% on next S$100K. Established companies receive the Partial Tax Exemption (PTE): 75% on first S$10K and 50% on next S$190K. Many activities qualify for further incentives (PIC successors, R&D deductions, IP regime). Singapore taxes on a territorial basis - foreign-sourced income is generally not taxed unless remitted into Singapore.
terms.example: A new startup with S$300K chargeable income pays approximately S$28K in tax in its first year (after applying SUTE), giving an effective rate of around 9.4%.

terms.title: GST
terms.shortDef: Singapore's value-added tax (currently 9%), applied to the sale of goods and services.
terms.definition: Goods and Services Tax (GST) is Singapore's value-added consumption tax. As of 2024, the rate is 9%. Registration is mandatory if your taxable turnover exceeds S$1 million in any 12-month period (retrospective basis), or if you reasonably expect it to exceed that threshold. Voluntary registration is possible if you make taxable supplies but are below the threshold - useful if your customers are GST-registered businesses (so they can claim input GST). GST returns are filed quarterly (monthly for some).
terms.example: An e-commerce business with S$1.2M turnover crosses the registration threshold and must register for GST within 30 days of the threshold being crossed. From the registration date, it must charge 9% GST on local sales.

terms.title: Pte Ltd
terms.shortDef: The most common Singapore company type - a separate legal entity with limited liability for shareholders.
terms.definition: A Private Company Limited by Shares (Pte Ltd) is the standard Singapore corporate structure. It is a separate legal entity from its shareholders, can own assets, sue and be sued, enter contracts in its own name, and shields shareholders from personal liability beyond their capital contribution. A Pte Ltd can have 1 to 50 shareholders, must have at least 1 director who is ordinarily resident in Singapore, and must appoint a corporate secretary within 6 months of incorporation. Most startups, family offices, and trading businesses incorporate as Pte Ltd.
terms.example: A foreign founder incorporating in Singapore typically forms a Pte Ltd, appoints a Singapore-resident nominee director, and holds 100% of the shares. The company can open a corporate bank account, hire employees, and operate as a separate legal person.

terms.title: Sole Proprietorship
terms.shortDef: An unincorporated business owned by a single individual, with no separation between owner and business.
terms.definition: A Sole Proprietorship is the simplest Singapore business structure: a single individual operating a business in their own name or under a registered business name. There is no legal separation between owner and business - profits are taxed as personal income, and the owner has unlimited personal liability for business debts. Sole proprietorships can only be registered by Singapore citizens, PRs, or EntrePass holders, and the registration is renewed annually. Suitable for very small, low-risk operations; unsuitable for raising investment or scaling.
terms.example: A freelance designer who registers their business name 'Studio X' as a sole proprietorship pays personal income tax on profits (up to 24% top marginal rate) and is personally liable for any business debt.

terms.title: LLP
terms.shortDef: A partnership where each partner has limited personal liability, used for professional service firms.
terms.definition: A Limited Liability Partnership (LLP) is a hybrid business structure combining the flexibility of a partnership with the limited liability of a company. Partners are not personally liable for business debts beyond their capital contribution, except for their own wrongful acts. LLPs are taxed at the partner level (transparent for tax purposes) and require at least 2 partners. They are commonly used by law firms, accounting firms, and consulting practices.
terms.example: Two consultants forming an LLP enjoy partnership-style profit sharing while shielding their personal assets from liabilities arising from the firm's contracts.

terms.title: VCC
terms.shortDef: A flexible fund vehicle introduced by MAS in 2020, designed to host investment funds with variable share capital.
terms.definition: The Variable Capital Company (VCC) is a Singapore corporate structure designed specifically for investment funds. Introduced by MAS in 2020, it allows funds to issue and redeem shares without shareholder approval, pay dividends out of capital, and segregate assets and liabilities across sub-funds (umbrella structure). VCCs must appoint a MAS-licensed fund manager and a Singapore-based corporate secretary, and may qualify for fund tax incentives under Section 13O or 13U of the Income Tax Act.
terms.example: A family office structures a multi-asset fund as an umbrella VCC with 3 sub-funds (equities, fixed income, alternatives), each ring-fenced for liability. The VCC appoints a licensed fund manager and applies for the 13O tax incentive.

terms.title: Section 13O
terms.shortDef: A Singapore tax incentive granting tax exemption to qualifying onshore funds managed by Singapore-based fund managers.
terms.definition: Section 13O of the Income Tax Act provides tax exemption on specified income (primarily investment income) for qualifying onshore funds. The fund must be a Singapore-incorporated entity (commonly a VCC), managed by a Singapore-based fund manager licensed or exempted by MAS, with minimum AUM of S$10 million within 24 months and a minimum local business spend (currently S$200K). 13O is the smaller-fund counterpart to Section 13U.
terms.example: A family office establishes a single-family fund as a VCC with S$15M of family capital, appoints a Singapore licensed fund manager, and applies for 13O - exempting the fund's investment returns from Singapore tax.

terms.title: Section 13U
terms.fullName: Section 13U Enhanced Tier Fund Tax Incentive
terms.shortDef: An enhanced Singapore fund tax incentive for larger institutional funds, with higher AUM thresholds and broader eligibility.
terms.definition: Section 13U is the enhanced tier of the Singapore fund tax incentive regime. Designed for larger institutional and professional funds, it has a higher minimum AUM (S$50M committed at application, with build-up over time), higher minimum local business spend (S$200K), and at least 3 investment professionals based in Singapore. 13U funds may be onshore or offshore, with greater flexibility on investor profile compared to 13O.
terms.example: A multi-family office structures a S$100M fund as a VCC, hires 3 investment professionals in Singapore, and applies for 13U - giving it tax exemption on investment income with broader investor eligibility.

terms.title: Family Office
terms.shortDef: A private wealth management entity that manages the investments and affairs of one or more wealthy families.
terms.definition: A Family Office (FO) is a private entity that manages the investment, tax, succession, and lifestyle affairs of one (Single Family Office) or several (Multi Family Office) high-net-worth families. In Singapore, family offices commonly structure their investment vehicles as VCCs and apply for Section 13O or 13U tax incentives. MAS does not license SFOs that manage only their own family's assets, but MFOs typically require a Capital Markets Services (CMS) licence as fund managers.
terms.example: A Gulf family with US$80M in liquid assets establishes a Singapore SFO with a VCC fund vehicle, applies for 13U, and obtains EP visas for the family principal and investment lead.

terms.title: EP
terms.shortDef: Singapore's main work visa for professionals, managers, and executives earning above the qualifying salary threshold.
terms.definition: The Employment Pass (EP) is the primary work visa issued by the Ministry of Manpower (MOM) to foreign professionals, managers, executives, and specialists. The minimum qualifying salary is S$5,000/month (S$5,500 for financial services), rising with age. Applicants must also pass the COMPASS framework - a points-based assessment of salary, qualifications, diversity, and skills. The EP is initially valid for up to 2 years and renewable for up to 3 years. EP holders can sponsor dependants under the Dependant's Pass.
terms.example: A 35-year-old foreign engineering director earning S$10K/month is eligible for an EP if they meet COMPASS (typically a score of 40+ across the 4 attributes).

terms.title: S Pass
terms.shortDef: A mid-skilled work pass for technical staff, with quota and levy requirements on the employer.
terms.definition: The S Pass is a Singapore work pass for mid-skilled foreign workers (typically technicians, supervisors, or junior specialists) earning above S$3,150/month (rising with age). Unlike the EP, the S Pass is subject to a sub-Dependency Ratio Ceiling quota on the employer's foreign workforce, and the employer must pay a monthly levy. Employers in services, manufacturing, and construction face different quota and levy schedules.
terms.example: A small services company with 10 local staff can hire up to about 1 S Pass holder under the 10% sub-DRC quota, paying the standard S$330-S$650/month levy.

terms.title: Tech.Pass
terms.shortDef: A specialised pass for established tech founders, leaders, and experts entering Singapore's tech ecosystem.
terms.definition: Tech.Pass is a specialised 2-year work pass administered by EDB for top-tier tech professionals - founders, senior leaders, and experts. Unlike the EP, Tech.Pass is not tied to a single employer; holders can run a business, work for multiple employers, mentor, invest, or lecture. Eligibility requires meeting at least 2 of 3 criteria: fixed monthly salary of at least S$30K in the year before application, leadership of a tech company with at least US$500M valuation or US$30M raised, or 5+ years of experience in a leading product/service with 100K+ users or US$100M+ revenue.
terms.example: A founder who previously led product at a US$1B-valued startup and earns US$25K/month qualifies for Tech.Pass on the leadership and salary criteria, allowing them to run a Singapore startup without an EP.

terms.title: ONE Pass
terms.shortDef: Singapore's most prestigious work pass for top-earning global talent, valid for 5 years and not tied to an employer.
terms.definition: The Overseas Networks & Expertise (ONE) Pass is a 5-year, employer-agnostic work pass for top-tier global talent. Eligibility requires either a fixed monthly salary of at least S$30,000 in the year before application (with a current or future Singapore employer), or outstanding achievements in arts, sciences, sports, academia, or research. ONE Pass holders can concurrently start, operate, and work for multiple companies in Singapore, and bring dependants on Dependant's Passes.
terms.example: A senior tech executive earning S$35K/month qualifies for ONE Pass, allowing them to be employed by their main company while concurrently advising other Singapore startups, with no need to renew the pass when changing roles.

terms.title: PEP
terms.shortDef: A 3-year employer-agnostic pass for high-earning EP holders or overseas professionals.
terms.definition: The Personalised Employment Pass (PEP) is a 3-year work pass for high-earning professionals, not tied to a specific employer. Eligibility requires a fixed monthly salary of at least S$22,500 (overseas applicants) or S$18,000 (existing EP holders). PEP holders can change jobs without re-applying, take time between jobs (with a 6-month maximum unemployment), and engage in business activities. The PEP cannot be renewed - holders must transition to ONE Pass or revert to a regular EP at end of term.
terms.example: An EP-holding finance director earning S$20K/month upgrades to a PEP, allowing them to leave their employer to start a fund management firm without re-applying for a new pass.

terms.title: Nominee Director
terms.shortDef: A Singapore-resident individual appointed to satisfy the local director requirement, without operational involvement.
terms.definition: A Singapore Pte Ltd must have at least one director who is ordinarily resident in Singapore. When the founders are foreign, a Nominee Director (also called a Resident Director) is appointed to meet this requirement. The nominee acts as a director under the Companies Act with full statutory duties but typically does not operate or sign for the business beyond compliance matters. Nominee directors are usually provided by Corporate Service Providers (CSPs) and require KYC, indemnification, and a service agreement.
terms.example: A foreign founder incorporating a Singapore Pte Ltd appoints Karman as nominee director to meet the resident director requirement, while the founder retains full beneficial ownership and operational control.

terms.title: Corporate Secretary
terms.shortDef: A statutory officer appointed within 6 months of incorporation, responsible for the company's compliance filings.
terms.definition: Under Singapore's Companies Act, every Pte Ltd must appoint a corporate secretary within 6 months of incorporation. The corporate secretary is a statutory officer responsible for maintaining the company's registers, preparing AGM and director resolutions, lodging ACRA filings (annual return, change of officer particulars, share allotments), and ensuring the company complies with the Companies Act. The role can be outsourced to an ACRA-Registered Filing Agent.
terms.example: Karman serves as corporate secretary for hundreds of Singapore companies, handling all ACRA filings, statutory registers, AGM/EGM documents, and reminders of upcoming deadlines.

terms.title: Registered Address
terms.shortDef: The official Singapore address where ACRA and IRAS send statutory notices to a company.
terms.definition: Every Singapore company must maintain a registered office address in Singapore - a physical (not P.O. Box) address where statutory mail from ACRA, IRAS, and other government agencies is sent. The address must be open to the public for at least 3 hours per business day. Many companies, especially foreign-founded ones, use a registered address service provided by their corporate secretary or filing agent rather than renting their own office.
terms.example: A foreign founder's new Pte Ltd uses Karman's office at 60 Paya Lebar Road as its registered address - government mail is received there and forwarded to the founder digitally.

terms.title: Share Capital
terms.shortDef: The total value of shares issued to shareholders - Singapore companies have no minimum capital requirement.
terms.definition: Share capital is the total value of shares a company has issued to its shareholders. Singapore has no minimum share capital requirement - a Pte Ltd can be incorporated with as little as S$1 paid-up capital. Share capital can be increased through new share allotments and decreased through buybacks or capital reductions (which require court or solvency-statement procedures). Some scenarios (banking licences, EP applications, fund management licences) effectively require higher capital.
terms.example: A startup typically incorporates with S$1,000 paid-up capital divided into 1,000 ordinary shares of S$1 each. Later seed/Series A rounds increase capital through new share allotments at higher prices.

terms.title: KYC
terms.shortDef: The process of verifying the identity of customers and beneficial owners before establishing a business relationship.
terms.definition: Know Your Customer (KYC) is the process by which regulated entities (banks, fund managers, corporate service providers, payment institutions) verify the identity of their clients and the ultimate beneficial owners of corporate clients. In Singapore, KYC requirements are driven by the MAS Notice on AML/CFT, ACRA's Filing Agent regulations, and global FATF standards. KYC typically requires identity documents, proof of address, source of funds documentation, and ongoing monitoring.
terms.example: Before incorporating a Pte Ltd, Karman performs KYC on each director, shareholder, and beneficial owner - collecting passport copies, proof of address, and a screening check for sanctions and PEP exposure.

terms.title: Beneficial Owner
terms.shortDef: The natural person who ultimately owns or controls a company, even through layers of intermediaries.
terms.definition: A Beneficial Owner (or Ultimate Beneficial Owner, UBO) is the natural person who ultimately owns or exercises control over a company. Under Singapore's Companies Act, all companies must maintain a Register of Registrable Controllers - tracking individuals who hold 25%+ of shares or voting rights, or who otherwise have significant control. ACRA requires the register to be kept up to date and made available to law enforcement on request. The threshold and disclosure rules align with FATF and OECD standards on beneficial ownership transparency.
terms.example: A Pte Ltd held 100% by a BVI company, which is in turn held 100% by an individual, must register the individual as the ultimate beneficial controller - not the BVI entity.
